other_side: The loudest economic claim in today's brief is NLW's — that the whole US economy now rests on Anthropic and OpenAI revenue rising and investors continuing to fund the buildout, which is why the Fable shutdown is so dangerous. Writing the day before, Zitron argues the dependency is the danger: both labs have just filed to go public precisely because they 'burn billions of dollars a year and have no path to profitability,' racing for what he calls 'exit liquidity' before the bill lands. The trillion-dollar valuations NLW says are 'now in question' were, on his telling, never sound to begin with. Where NLW sees a national asset a clumsy government must stop endangering, Zitron sees the bubble itself — and a government merely poking it a little early.
truth_beneath: Strip the rhetoric and both men orbit one variable: whether the frontier labs have a path to profit or merely a path to more capital. They accept the same facts — Anthropic and OpenAI have both filed to go public, both burn billions a year, and trillion-dollar valuations now hang on events neither company controls. The empirical question that will resolve is whether the flotation filings show margins bending toward profit or losses widening; the audited prospectus financials are the observable that settles it. For NLW to be vindicated, revenue must scale and the buildout stay funded, so 'the economy rests on this' holds. For Zitron, the filings must expose the dog — no margin, endless burn, exit liquidity dressed as growth. Both are polemicists: NLW sells optimism and the anti-doom case, Zitron sells bubble-certainty and anti-hype. The signal is where they agree despite opposite priors — both now call the valuation precarious. Watch the prospectus.
